question_text: What is the output of the following JavaScript code? Code: const nums = [1, 2, 3]; const result = nums.map(n => n * 2).filter(n => n > 3); console.log(result);

Which change best fixes the bug in this React component? Code: function Counter() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); function inc() { setCount(count + 1); setCount(count + 1); } return <button onClick={inc}>{count}</button>; }
option_d: Use useEffect to update count after render

question_text: What is the most likely problem with this Node.js route? Code: app.get("/users/:id", async (req, res) => { const user = await db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ${req.params.id}`); res.json(user.rows[0]); });
option_a: The route should use POST instead of GET
option_b: The query is vulnerable to SQL injection

question_text: What will this Python code print? Code: items = [] for i in range(3): items.append(lambda: i) print([fn() for fn in items])
Which SQL result is produced by this query? Code: -- table orders: id, status -- rows: (1, "paid"), (2, "pending"), (3, "paid") SELECT status, COUNT(*) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY status;
option_c: Three rows, one per order id
option_d: The query fails because COUNT needs WHERE

question_text: What does this C++ code print? Code: #include <iostream> using namespace std; int main() { int x = 5; int& y = x; y = 10; cout << x; }
Which statement best describes the issue in this Express middleware order? Code: app.get("/profile", profileHandler); app.use(authMiddleware); app.get("/settings", settingsHandler);
option_c: /profile is registered before authMiddleware, so it may bypass auth
option_d: Express does not allow middleware after routes

question_text: What is the output of this Java code? Code: String a = new String("test"); String b = new String("test"); System.out.println(a == b); System.out.println(a.equals(b));
Which option is the best fix for this async JavaScript bug? Code: async function load() { const data = fetch("/api/items"); console.log(data.length); }
option_a: Add await before fetch and parse the response before using data

question_text: What is wrong with this React list rendering code? Code: {users.map((user, index) => ( <UserCard key={index} user={user} /> ))}
option_b: index as key can cause incorrect UI updates when list order changes
option_c: key must be passed as user prop
option_d: React requires a for loop instead of map

question_text: What will this TypeScript code report? Code: let value: string | number = "42"; value = 42; console.log(value.toUpperCase());
option_c: TypeScript reports an error because number may not have toUpperCase
option_d: It compiles because union types allow all methods

question_text: Which database concept prevents this double-spend style bug most directly? Code: balance = SELECT balance FROM accounts WHERE id = 1; if balance >= 100: UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;
option_b: Transaction with locking or conditional update

question_text: What does this Python function return for factorial(4)? Code: def factorial(n): if n == 0: return 1 return n * factorial(n - 1) print(factorial(4))
Which HTTP status code is most appropriate for this situation? Code: POST /login Body: { "email": "wrong@example.com", "password": "bad" } Result: credentials are invalid
What is the output of this JavaScript code? Code: console.log(typeof null); console.log(Array.isArray([]));
Which option best describes this CSS behavior? Code: .box { width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 5px solid black; box-sizing: border-box; }
option_a: The visible box width remains 200px including padding and border
option_d: Border is drawn outside the browser viewport

question_text: What will this MongoDB query return? Code: db.users.find({ age: { $gte: 18 }, active: true })
option_a: Users younger than 18 who are active
option_b: Users at least 18 years old and active

question_text: Which option is the strongest fix for this security issue? Code: localStorage.setItem("accessToken", token); // token is long-lived and gives full account access
option_a: Store the token in a global variable forever
option_b: Use short-lived access tokens and safer refresh/session handling
option_c: Encode the token with Base64 before storing it

question_text: What is the output of this C++ code? Code: #include <iostream> using namespace std; void change(int x) { x = 20; } int main() { int a = 10; change(a); cout << a; }
Which option best explains why this API design is risky? Code: app.delete("/users/:id", (req, res) => { db.deleteUser(req.params.id); res.sendStatus(204); });
option_b: The route deletes without checking authentication and authorization
